name: Turmeric

book_claims.claim: Curcumin has anti-inflammatory, antioxidant and anti-cancer effects, particularly relevant to colon cancer prevention
book_claims.mechanism: Curcumin affects gene activity and induces apoptosis (programmed death) of cancer cells. Inhibitory effects against colon, skin and oral cancer in rodents.

book_claims.claim: Six months of curcumin + quercetin treatment resulted in significant reduction of colon polyp number and size
book_claims.mechanism: Synergistic effect of curcumin and quercetin on colon polyp regression

book_claims.claim: May play a role in dementia prevention by inhibiting amyloid plaque formation
book_claims.mechanism: Curcumin inhibits formation of amyloid plaques associated with Alzheimer's disease

supplementary_research.finding: Hewlings & Kalman (2017) review in Foods: curcumin at doses of 1000mg/day significantly reduced CRP and other inflammatory biomarkers in multiple RCTs. Anti-inflammatory mechanism operates through inhibition of NF-kB, COX-2, and LOX pathways. Most benefits require enhanced bioavailability formulations (piperine, lipid nanoparticles, or phytosomal forms).

Sahebkar et al. (2019) meta-analysis of 8 RCTs: curcumin supplementation significantly reduced serum CRP (WMD: -1.55 mg/L), TNF-alpha, and IL-6 levels. Effects were most pronounced in participants with elevated baseline inflammation, supporting the book's anti-inflammatory claims.

Examine's analysis of 282+ studies confirms curcumin reliably reduces inflammatory markers (IL-6, CRP, TNF-alpha) and may modestly improve depression symptoms (comparable to fluoxetine in one RCT). Notes that bioavailability is the key limitation -- standard curcumin has less than 1% oral bioavailability; piperine co-administration increases absorption by 2000%. Cancer prevention evidence is strong in vitro but limited in human trials.

Small et al. (2018) double-blind RCT in the American Journal of Geriatric Psychiatry (PMID: 29246725): 90mg bioavailable curcumin (Theracurmin) twice daily for 18 months significantly improved memory performance and reduced amyloid/tau accumulation in non-demented adults, measured by FDDNP-PET scans. Supports the book's claim about dementia prevention potential.

Shoba et al. (1998, classic study PMID 9619120) demonstrated piperine increases curcumin bioavailability by 2000% in humans. Confirmed by multiple subsequent studies. Di Silvestro et al. (2012) showed that even low-dose lipidated curcumin (80mg/day) reduced plasma beta-amyloid, salivary amylase, and plasma sGP-E selectin.

culinary_pairings.source: Traditional Ayurvedic medicine and Indian cuisine; piperine synergy for curcumin absorption
Classic curry base; both rhizomes with complementary anti-inflammatory pathways
Thai yellow curry, Indonesian jamu (turmeric tonic)
Core Indian spice blend; foundational curry powder combination
Dal and chana masala; turmeric is essential in nearly all Indian legume dishes
Aloo gobi; cauliflower readily absorbs turmeric color and flavor
Golden milk (haldi doodh); warming spice blends

seasonality: Year-round as dried powder. Fresh turmeric rhizomes peak availability in late autumn to winter harvest (October-March). Store dried powder away from light to preserve curcuminoid content.

nutrient_highlights.amount_per_100g: 3,000-5,000mg (3-5% of dried turmeric powder by weight)
nutrient_highlights.bioavailability_notes: Extremely poor bioavailability (<1%) without enhancers. Piperine (black pepper) increases absorption ~2000%. Fat co-ingestion also improves absorption. Lipid-based and nanoparticle formulations under study.
Second most abundant curcuminoid; similar bioavailability limitations as curcumin
Third curcuminoid; may have distinct anti-cancer properties

nutrient_highlights.amount_per_100g: Present in turmeric essential oil (2-5% of rhizome)
nutrient_highlights.bioavailability_notes: Volatile compound in essential oil fraction; may enhance curcumin absorption and has independent neuroprotective activity in preclinical models
Non-heme iron; absorption enhanced by vitamin C co-ingestion
High content but consumed in small quantities as a spice; contributes modestly to daily intake

synergies.description: Piperine in black pepper doubles blood curcumin levels by improving absorption
Quercetin (in capers) + curcumin resulted in significant colon polyp reduction in pilot study
